Fixing 'Blocked request' When Exposing a Nuxt 3 Dev Server Through ngrok
Fixing 'Blocked request' when exposing a Nuxt 3 dev server through ngrok
If you've been using ngrok to expose your local dev server, after upgrading to Nuxt 3 (or bumping Vite past 6.x), you've probably hit this message:

Blocked request. This host ("xxxx-xx-xx-xx-xx.ngrok-free.app") is not allowed.
To allow this host, add "xxxx-xx-xx-xx-xx.ngrok-free.app" to `server.allowedHosts` in vite.config.js.
Blocked request. This host ("xxxx-xx-xx-xx-xx.ngrok-free.app") is not allowed.
To allow this host, add "xxxx-xx-xx-xx-xx.ngrok-free.app" to `server.allowedHosts` in vite.config.js.

? Then it clicked — Nuxt 3's client side actually runs on Vite, and this host check is a security measure Vite added in 6.x to prevent

ngrok's free tier gives you a new subdomain every time you restart the tunnel — so you don't want to come back and edit this every time.

If you also use tunnels like Cloudflare Tunnel or Tailscale Funnel, add their domains to the same array.

I really don't like writing it this way — it nukes the whole point of Vite's host check. Yes, the dev server only runs locally, but once you've fired up ngrok or Cloudflare Tunnel, your dev server is effectively public. Spending ten more seconds on a whitelist is worth it.

A quick note on fixing "Blocked request. This host is not allowed" when exposing a Nuxt 3 dev server through ngrok — including why Nuxt points you at a `vite.config.js` that doesn't exist, where to actually put `server.allowedHosts` in `nuxt.config.ts`, how to use the leading-dot wildcard to cover all ngrok subdomains, and why setting `allowedHosts: true` is a bad idea.
